title: A Robot Walks into a Bar: Can Language Models Serve as Creativity Support Tools for Comedy? An Evaluation of LLMs’ Humour Alignment with Comedians
venue: The 2024 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT ’24), June 3–6, 2024, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
research_questions: What are the current state, use-cases, and limitations of generally available instruction-tuned LLMs as comedy-writing support tools?
How do professional comedians evaluate LLMs as creativity support tools for comedy writing, including via the Creativity Support Index?
How do comedians experience and interpret LLM problems around stereotypes, censorship or moderation, missing context, and homogenisation?
What ethical concerns do comedians raise about using AI for comedy, especially around bias, copyright, data ownership, and value alignment?

datasets_used.name: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts
datasets_used.size: 20 professional comedians; 4 focus groups; 45-minute writing sessions

methods_proposed.name: AI x Comedy mixed-method workshop protocol
methods_proposed.summary: The study combines a roughly 45-minute comedy-writing exercise with LLMs, Likert-scale and Creativity Support Index surveys, free-form survey questions, and one-hour focus groups with professional comedians. Focus-group transcripts are analyzed using constant comparison analysis with open coding and thematic grouping.

methods_proposed.name: Community-based cultural value alignment for humour and comedy
methods_proposed.summary: The paper proposes moving from global value alignment toward alignment with specific comedy communities and intended audiences, using community data, feedback, norms, and governance. It also argues for incorporating relational context and giving artists more control over moderation decisions.

humor_theories.detail: The introduction cites incongruity as a basic element of humour, where the setup points in one direction and the punch line in another, and later frames producing and resolving incongruity as a task with human-level difficulty.
Mentioned in a footnote as an alternative humour theory of tension and release.
Mentioned in a footnote as an alternative humour theory in which we laugh at others’ misfortunes to feel better about ourselves.
Mentioned in a footnote as an alternative humour theory.
The discussion cites Winters’ claim that humour’s frame-shifting prerequisite makes it difficult for machines and supports the view that humour can be an AI-complete problem.

evaluation_methods.detail: Twenty professional comedians used generally available instruction-tuned LLMs in a comedy-writing session, then completed Likert-scale questions and a Creativity Support Index questionnaire assessing Exploration, Expressiveness, Immersion, Enjoyment, Results Worth Effort, and Collaboration.

The authors manually verified transcripts and performed constant comparison analysis: sentence-by-sentence open coding, grouping codes into themes, and identifying themes coherent across four focus groups.

headline_results.finding: The overall Creativity Support Index score for LLMs as comedy-writing tools was described as mediocre.
headline_results.dataset: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts

headline_results.finding: The study did not observe a significant change in Creativity Support Index among the models between the August and December workshops.
headline_results.metric: Creativity Support Index by workshop period
headline_results.dataset: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts

headline_results.finding: The participant pool consisted of professional comedians split evenly between an in-person Edinburgh Festival Fringe workshop and online workshops.
headline_results.value: 20 professional comedians; 10 in person at the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, 10 online
headline_results.dataset: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts

headline_results.finding: A recurring qualitative result was that multiple comedians found LLM outputs bland or generic.
headline_results.dataset: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts

headline_results.finding: Only part of the participant pool answered the Likert question about whether they collaborated with AI.
headline_results.metric: Survey response count for “Collaborated with AI”
headline_results.dataset: AI x Comedy workshop writing-session outputs, survey results, and focus-group transcripts

key_findings: Comedians saw some practical value in LLMs for quick first drafts, structure, brainstorming, criticism, choreography assistance, translation, and historical lookup.
Many participants judged generated comedy to be poor, bland, generic, and dependent on the human writer to supply the humorous elements.

Participants said moderation and safety filtering limited creative agency, blocked common comedy topics such as sexually suggestive material, dark humor, and offensive jokes, and acted as a form of censorship.
Participants reported that LLMs reflected dominant Western, white, heteronormative, and male norms, and that attempts to prompt non-dominant identities often produced only shallow changes.
Comedians identified fundamental limitations of LLMs for comedy: lack of lived experience, perspective, audience and venue context, delivery, and surprise.
Participants raised concerns about copyrighted training data, possible plagiarism, lack of diversity in training data, licensing, compensation, and transparency.
limitations: Participants were recruited because they already used AI in their artistic processes, which the authors state likely biased the results.
The study included only 20 professional comedians, with 10 in person and 10 online.
The authors did not collect demographic data because participant anonymity was an ethics requirement, limiting demographic analysis of opinions.
The wording of some background questionnaire items could be considered potentially biasing.
The focus-group questions were leading and could have biased respondents.
The study used generally available instruction-tuned models and notes that more complex prompting strategies could have produced higher-quality outputs.
The authors explicitly state that they do not generalize their findings beyond comedy.
The paper does not propose technical solutions for incorporating broader relational context into LLMs.
safety_ethics_notes: The study was approved by the authors’ institutional ethics board, obtained active consent, allowed withdrawal without prejudice, paid participants £300 for 3 hours, and anonymised participants. The paper includes a warning that it may contain offensive language and discusses self-harm; the authors also discuss adverse impacts including reputation damage for comedians using AI, accidental publication of comedians’ work, and advertising LLM use as writing tools.

artifacts.other: The paper states that full outputs of the writing sessions, individual survey results, and raw focus-group transcripts will be shared in anonymised form as supplementary material once the work is published.

human_samples.population: Professional comedians/performers who perform live shows in front of audiences and who already use AI in their artistic process.
human_samples.recruitment: Authors reached out to comedians performing at Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023 or in their network; they attempted to recruit a diverse pool along linguistic, cultural, gender, sexual, national, and racial dimensions. Participants registered through Prolific via an allowlist and were paid £300 for a 3-hour workshop.
human_samples.geography: 10 participated in person at Edinburgh Festival Fringe 2023; 10 participated online in three workshops with 3, 4, and 3 participants. No demographic data were collected because participant anonymity was an ethics requirement.
human_samples.observations: Four workshops; around 45 minutes of individual LLM-based comedy writing per participant; three post-writing surveys; one-hour focus-group discussions. Only 10 participants responded to the Likert item “Collaborated with AI.”
human_samples.detail: Participants were invited to use the language(s) they felt most comfortable with. The paper reports a 5-level Likert survey, a Creativity Support Index questionnaire, free-form survey questions, and focus-group transcripts/quotes from anonymised participants p1–p20.

human_samples.population: Paper authors / researchers conducting qualitative analysis.
human_samples.observations: Focus-group transcripts were automatically transcribed using Google Meet speech recognition tools, manually verified against moderator notes, anonymised, and analysed by constant comparison analysis with sentence-by-sentence open coding, grouping into themes, and identification of themes coherent across focus groups.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number of coders, intercoder reliability, or a full codebook; it states that authors independently reviewed transcripts to remove personally identifiable information.

judges.name: Professional comedian workshop participants
judges.detail: Participants interacted with LLMs and provided the primary evaluative evidence through Likert ratings, Creativity Support Index responses, free-form survey comments, and focus-group discussion. They knew they were using AI tools.

judges.name: Paper authors / qualitative analysts
judges.detail: Authors manually verified transcripts, anonymised them, and performed constant comparison analysis to derive qualitative themes reported in Section 4.

judges.name: Creativity Support Index (CSI)
judges.detail: CSI was computed from the participant questionnaire to produce quantitative scores such as μ = 54.6, σ = 18.1. The metric is a formula applied to human survey responses, not an LLM judge.

generation_selection.detail: The 10 in-person participants spent around 45 minutes individually using study-provided ChatGPT-3.5 through a plain-text interface similar to ChatGPT. The exercise was open-ended; no fixed prompt set, per-item candidate count, sampling count, or best-of-N selection protocol was reported.

The 10 online participants, split across three workshops of 3, 4, and 3, used their preferred model via personal accounts: ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4, or Google Bard powered by Gemini Pro. Interaction was open-ended and participant-directed; no per-prompt candidate counts or formal selection rules were reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT-3.5
model_provenance.detail: In the first workshop, participants were provided access to ChatGPT-3.5 served via a plain-text interface similar to ChatGPT. In later online workshops, some participants used ChatGPT-3.5 via personal accounts. Exact model snapshot/API version was not reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Used by some online participants through their personal account. The paper does not report the exact GPT-4/ChatGPT-4 snapshot or how many participants used it.

model_provenance.name: Google Bard powered by Gemini Pro
model_provenance.detail: Used by some online participants through their personal account. The paper identifies Bard as powered by Gemini Pro and specifies “December 2023 version,” but not an exact snapshot.

model_provenance.name: Google Meet speech recognition tools
model_provenance.detail: Used to automatically transcribe focus-group audio before manual verification; not an evaluated comedy-writing model.

inference_budgets.scope: LLM comedy-writing sessions across all workshops
inference_budgets.detail: Participants spent around 45 minutes using an LLM in an open-ended writing exercise. The paper does not report number of prompts, number of generations, token limits, context-window limits, temperature, decoding parameters, model-call counts, or inference cost.

inference_budgets.scope: Google Meet speech-recognition transcription of focus groups
inference_budgets.detail: Focus groups were recorded as audio, automatically transcribed with Google Meet speech recognition tools, manually verified, and then the audio/video recordings were destroyed. Exact transcription model, settings, and processing budget were not reported.

human_baselines.description: The study did not include a matched human-only writing condition or a controlled comparison against comedy material written without AI. Participants discussed differences between LLM use and working alone, with other comedians, or with resources such as Wikipedia/Google search, but these were qualitative reflections rather than a directly comparable baseline.
human_baselines.detail: The primary quantitative evidence is participant evaluation after using LLM tools; no parallel human baseline was scored under the same CSI/Likert setup.

mitigations: The evaluation was based on newly conducted 2023 workshops, surveys, and focus groups rather than a fixed public benchmark.
There were no ground-truth benchmark items or held-out test labels used to score model accuracy.
contamination_risk.basis: This is an HCI/user-study evaluation of LLMs as comedy-writing support tools, not a public benchmark evaluation. The paper discusses copyrighted training data and possible plagiarism concerns, but it does not discuss benchmark contamination because no legacy benchmark is used.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs were the writing tools being evaluated. The paper’s conclusions are based on human participant surveys/focus groups, CSI computation, and author qualitative analysis; no LLM was used to judge humor quality or determine the headline claims.

reporting_gaps: Exact ChatGPT-3.5 and ChatGPT-4 model snapshots, API versions, and system prompts are not reported.
The paper does not report how many online participants used each model, nor participant-level model assignments.
The access details for the study-provided ChatGPT-3.5 plain-text interface are not fully specified, including whether it used an API and which backend snapshot was served.
Number of prompts, number of model calls, number of generated outputs, candidate counts, and any best-of-N or selection procedure are not reported.
Token budgets, context-window limits, sampling/decoding parameters such as temperature or top-p, and inference costs are not reported.
The paper does not provide numerical response counts for all Likert items in the text; it notes only that 10 participants answered “Collaborated with AI.”
No demographic data were collected because participant anonymity was required, limiting demographic analysis.
The qualitative coding procedure does not report number of coders, intercoder agreement, adjudication process, or a complete codebook.

Raw writing-session outputs, individual survey results, and raw focus-group transcripts were not included in the paper text; the paper states they would be shared in anonymised form as supplementary material once published.
There is no directly comparable human-only baseline condition.
